channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Turtles Are Really Weird Animals. Where Did They Come From?
item.description: The origin of turtles has puzzled scientists. A new study provides more evidence that these shelled reptiles share a common ancestor with crocodiles and birds.
item.dc:creator: Sara Novak
item.media:description: A new study suggests turtles belong to a group of reptiles called archosauromorphs, which also includes ancient birds, crocodiles, pterosaurs and dinosaurs, and may share a common ancestor with them.

item.title: NASA Aims to Catch a Falling Space Telescope and Push It Back Up
item.description: A rescue mission will soon be underway for NASA’s Swift telescope.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory being ferried to a higher orbit over Earth by the start-up Katalyst Space Technologies’s Link spacecraft.

item.title: This Trap Wants Ants to Take a Bite
item.description: Named for an ancient Roman crossbow-like weapon, the newly found “ballista” spider uses a springy snare to catch prey.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.media:credit: Ajay Narendra et al., Current Biology
item.media:description: A newly described Australian ballista spider waiting to catch an ant in its snare.

item.title: How a Bird’s Habitat Can Change Its Song
item.description: For the Bachman’s sparrow, whether a song is passed to the next generation could depend, in part, on the wind and trees.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: Why the Venezuelan Earthquakes Happened, and What to Expect Next
item.description: Twin earthquakes like those that ripped through the region are unusual but not unheard of. Scientists are already gathering data needed for a more detailed picture.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews
item.media:description: Emergency workers at the site of a collapsed building in Caracas on Thursday.

item.title: Tickled Apes Reveal the Rhythmic Roots of Laughter
item.description: A study of chimps, gorillas and other great apes, including human children, sheds light on how laughter has evolved.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Emil Lippe for The New York Times
item.media:description: All the planet’s great apes laugh, and they often do so in the same kind of regular, repeating rhythm that humans do, according to a new study.

item.title: These Birds’ Backflips Are Fueled by Sugar
item.description: An ancient dietary change made the manakin’s flashy courtship display possible, a new study suggests.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: The Ebola Outbreak’s Central Mystery: Where Did This Virus Come From?
item.description: Scientists believe that the Bundibugyo virus persists in an animal species, occasionally spilling over into humans. But they have yet to identify the species.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: Fruit Fly Sperm Are Giant. How Do They Stay Untangled?
item.description: A fruit fly’s sperm are exceptionally long, and thousands are crammed in together. The physics of this presents a packing nightmare.
item.dc:creator: Monique Brouillette

item.title: Another Trump Administration Payment to Stop Offshore Wind Farm
item.description: It was the fourth such deal struck by the administration to get companies to forfeit their offshore wind leases.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: Duke Energy is one of the nation’s largest utilities. Here, a Duke Energy wind farm project in Rolling Hills, Wyoming.

item.title: Richard Scolyer, Cancer Expert Who ‘Became His Own Subject,’ Dies at 59
item.description: His lifesaving melanoma research in Australia illuminated the treatment he underwent for his own brain tumor, an ordeal he courageously shared with the public.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: Bianca De Marchi/AAP Image, via Reuters
item.media:description: Dr. Richard Scolyer in 2023. He became a high-profile public figure in Australia as he wrote and spoke about his experimental treatments for his own aggressive cancer.

item.title: Gregg Phillips Leaving FEMA
item.description: Gregg Phillips was in charge of the emergency response agency’s largest division and had come under scrutiny for a series of bizarre claims.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Scott Dance
item.media:description: Gregg Phillips was appointed to lead FEMA’s Office of Response and Recovery, with 1,000 employees and a budget of nearly $300 million.

item.title: Oil Industry Lawyers Fight a $50 Billion Climate Case in Oregon
item.description: The lawsuit aims to hold fossil fuel companies responsible for a heat wave in Portland five years ago. The industry says the case should be thrown out.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Michael Hanson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Richard Schechter, a lawyer for Multnomah County in Oregon, addressing the judge in court on Wednesday.

item.title: Climate Change Fueling Europe’s Ferocious Heat Wave, Scientists Find
item.description: A scientific analysis concluded that such high temperatures, across so much of the continent, would “not have been possible” without global warming.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: A shady passageway on Tuesday in Bordeaux, France, where temperatures reached 108 degrees Fahrenheit, or roughly 42 Celsius.

item.title: Trump Issues Order to Reduce Pesticides in Food as Kennedy Allies Fume
item.description: The order, which calls for studying the health risks of pesticides in the food supply, does not involve new federal funding, and does not call for regulations or legislation.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:description: President Trump held a Rose Garden dinner with American farmers on Thursday, the same day he issued an executive order aimed at reducing pesticides in the food supply.

item.title: Congo Ebola Crisis: Contact Tracing Is Dangerously Behind, Officials Warn
item.description: Most of the people testing positive for Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo are not on health workers’ radar, suggesting that contact tracing is lagging dangerously behind.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A community health worker taking the temperature of a man whose son is being treated for the virus near the city of Bunia in Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: Medical Journal Retracts Study Claiming Cancer Therapy Is More Effective When Given in the Morning
item.description: In a notice flagging a series of problems with a clinical trial, the journal Nature Medicine said its editors “no longer have confidence in the integrity of the results.”
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins
item.media:description: The journal Nature Medicine retracted a study that found a drastic benefit when cancer therapy was administered in the morning.

item.title: Global Warming Upends London Climate Week
item.description: The extreme heat smothering Europe has upended Climate Week in London.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: King Charles III at a London Climate Week reception on Wednesday at St. James’s Palace.

item.title: Inside the C.D.C.’s Mad Scramble to Meet Kennedy’s Demands
item.description: A cache of internal emails offers a look at the pressure the nation’s public health officials faced from the new health secretary in the early months of the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Senate Committee on Health, Education, Labor and Pensions
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr., seated, at a Senate confirmation hearing last year.

item.title: Chemours to Pay $450M in First Federal PFAS Settlement
item.description: The settlement addresses the dumping of PFAS “forever chemicals,” which have been linked to cancer and other risks, by Chemours in several states.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Ed Kashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Chemours plant in Fayetteville, N.C. Under a proposed settlement, Chemours would pay fines for discharging PFAS from plants in North Carolina, New Jersey and West Virginia.

item.title: Trump Administration Forms Partnership to Bank DNA From Imperiled Species
item.description: The government is teaming up with Colossal Biosciences, a private company that claims to have revived extinct dire wolves, to store samples from at-risk animals and plants.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer and Catrin Einhorn
item.media:description: The Frozen Zoo in San Diego, which has been preserving cells for over 50 years. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has announced a partnership with Colossal Biosciences for a similar effort.

item.title: Shortage of Chemotherapy Drugs Brings Rationing Fears for Cancer Patients
item.description: Doctors are contending with low supplies and unfilled orders of generic chemotherapy infusions that are central to the treatment of a long list of cancers.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Emil Lippe for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chemotherapy drugs, carboplatin and cisplatin, in the pharmaceutical storage room at the Center for Cancer and Blood Disorders in Fort Worth. There are shortages of both drugs.

item.title: Why Is Europe the Fastest-Warming Continent
item.description: The burning of fossil fuels is raising temperatures worldwide, but local factors, on land and at sea, determine which regions warm most rapidly.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: Morning commuters on the London Bridge on Wednesday, where temperatures reached 96 degrees Fahrenheit, the hottest recorded June day in the United Kingdom.

item.title: France Identifies Its First Case of Ebola
item.description: A doctor who had traveled to the Democratic Republic of Congo was infected, the French health ministry said. The authorities said the risk to the wider population was low.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers last month in the Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of an Ebola outbreak that has killed more than 200 people.

item.title: An Influx of Climate Cash
item.description: Many philanthropists are backing away from climate giving. But one is writing very big checks.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: Michael Bloomberg, the billionaire and former mayor of New York City, at a climate forum in London on Tuesday.

item.title: Europe’s Heat Has Scientists Asking: How Much Hotter Can It Get?
item.description: Records are being broken for the second time in a month, leading scientists to probe the upper limits of what the warming climate can dish out.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: A retirement home in Les Sorinières, near Nantes, France, on Monday, where temperatures reached 108 degrees Fahrenheit.

item.title: Ebola Symptoms in Current Outbreak May Be Milder Than in Previous Ones
item.description: That is good news for patients, but officials fear it will make controlling the spread of the disease harder.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A health worker checking people’s temperatures at an entry point to the city of Goma in the Democratic Republic of Congo in May.

item.title: Energy Dept. Promises $17.5 Billion in Loans for Nuclear Power
item.description: The federal government wants to help utilities buy large components for up to 10 large nuclear reactors. It’s unclear which, if any, companies might participate.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Kendrick Brinson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Vogtle nuclear power plant in Georgia is the only one with AP1000 reactors in the U.S. They generate enough electricity to power 1 million homes, but they took 15 years to build and cost $35 billion, double the initial estimates.

item.title: Former NOAA Employees Revive Climate.gov Web Site
item.description: The database of federal global warming research recreates a website that was closed amid the administration’s broad retreat from climate science.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration headquarters in Silver Spring, Md. The new site is an effort to preserve NOAA work previously available at climate.gov.

item.title: Europe Created Heat-Wave Protections. Now Comes the ‘Crash Test.’
item.description: Searing temperatures in Western Europe are drawing comparisons to 2003, when a deadly heat wave sparked a reckoning.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: A visitor to the Palace of Versailles in France on Monday, where temperatures exceeded 104 degrees Fahrenheit.

item.title: X-Ray Specs for the World’s Oldest, Sealed Letters
item.description: A team of historians, scientists and engineers has developed a portable X-ray scanner to study 4,000-year-old letters encased in clay envelopes.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Kornei
